skill.name: code-example-generator
skill.description: Generate teaching-focused code examples for tutorials, documentation, and lessons. The skill emphasizes specifications, learner level, validation, and clear explanations.
skill.summary: Create runnable, lesson-ready code examples that teach why a pattern works.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backticks, HTTP library references, and blocker patterns, but review found these are Markdown tutorial examples and ordinary prose. No executable scripts, prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious intent were found. The residual concern is low because the skill encourages validation of generated examples, which should occur only in a sandbox.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static external command findings are false positives. The flagged backticks are Markdown fences or inline code markers used to present examples, not runnable shell or Ruby execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are inside Markdown code fences or inline code formatting. No file in the scanned structure provides executable shell, Ruby, or script content.

low_findings.title: Network Library Appears Only in a Teaching Example
low_findings.description: The flagged HTTP reference is a short Python example using requests.get inside a Markdown snippet. It does not define a destination or perform network access by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network call is shown as illustrative tutorial code under a fenced example. There is no surrounding executable package, command hook, or exfiltration endpoint.

low_findings.title: High-Risk Blocker Patterns Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance blocker hits do not correspond to cryptographic APIs, system probes, or malicious reconnaissance. They appear in ordinary prose about syntax, validation, and explaining problems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no md5, sha1, DES, RC4, host enumeration, or environment probing at these lines. The lines are instructional prose headings and checklist text.

low_findings.title: Generated Code Validation Requires Sandboxing
low_findings.description: The skill recommends syntax and execution validation for generated examples. This is legitimate for education, but generated code should be run only in a constrained sandbox.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The validation guidance is explicit and legitimate, but any execution of generated code carries practical risk. The skill repeatedly names sandbox execution, which lowers the severity.

content.value_statement: Many code examples show syntax without building understanding. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create examples with context, validation, and learner fit.
actual_capabilities: Frames examples around a specification before generating code.
Targets examples to learner proficiency and prerequisite knowledge.
Chooses Python, TypeScript, or bilingual examples based on concept fit.
Uses show-then-explain structure for clearer learning.
Includes validation planning with normal, edge, and error cases.
Avoids overused toy examples when a realistic pattern is better.
limitations: It does not include executable helper scripts in the skill package.
It cannot guarantee generated code is correct without local validation.
It focuses on educational examples, not full application design.
It may need a clear lesson goal or specification for best results.

use_cases.title: Write Tutorial Examples
use_cases.description: Create clear examples that introduce one programming concept with context, tests, and explanation.

use_cases.title: Prepare Classroom Material
use_cases.description: Generate learner-appropriate examples for a lesson, workshop, or guided coding exercise.

use_cases.title: Improve Developer Docs
use_cases.description: Replace shallow snippets with realistic examples that explain when and why to use a pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Concept Example
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when introducing a single concept to new learners.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a beginner-friendly code example for [concept]. Use one main idea, simple vocabulary, and three short validation cases.

prompt_templates.title: Documentation Example
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when adding an example to product or API documentation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a documentation-ready example for [feature]. Show working usage first, then explain the problem it solves and common mistakes.

prompt_templates.title: Bilingual Learning Example
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when a concept transfers across Python and TypeScript.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create matching Python and TypeScript examples for [concept]. Highlight what transfers and what differs between the languages.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Validation Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when producing a complete lesson example from a specification.
prompt_templates.prompt: Using this specification, create a realistic example with success criteria, generated code structure, validation plan, edge cases, and learner-facing explanation.

output_examples.input: Generate a B1-level example for Python decorators.
output_examples.output: A realistic authentication decorator lesson with a working example, explanation, mistakes to avoid, and validation cases.

output_examples.input: Create a TypeScript example for generic functions.
output_examples.output: A focused example that shows one generic function pattern, explains why types matter, and includes practical test scenarios.

output_examples.input: Improve this tutorial snippet for async error handling.
output_examples.output: A revised lesson that separates async behavior from error handling, adds context, and provides normal and failure-path checks.

best_practices: Provide a clear concept, learner level, and desired language before invoking the skill.
Ask for one primary teaching goal per example.
Validate generated code in a sandbox before publishing it.
anti_patterns: Requesting a large multi-concept lesson without a specification.
Publishing generated code without syntax or execution checks.
Using toy examples when the audience needs production-relevant patterns.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates educational code examples with context, explanation, and validation guidance.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: The skill discusses Python and TypeScript, and it can choose one or both when appropriate.

faq.question: Can it write full applications?
faq.answer: It is designed for lesson examples, not full application architecture.

faq.question: Does it run generated code?
faq.answer: No. It recommends validation steps, but execution depends on the host tool and environment.

faq.question: Is it useful for documentation teams?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps turn shallow snippets into clearer examples for tutorials and API documentation.

faq.question: How should I get the best results?
faq.answer: Provide the concept, learner level, language, prerequisites, and any specification or success criteria.
